topic: A student finds out their scholarship was meant for someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official letter lay crumpled on my desk, the ink bleeding slightly where my thumb had pressed too hard. Every word swam before my eyes: "Recipient: Elias Vance." My name, Clara Hayes, was nowhere in the document. A cold, creeping sensation began in my stomach, tightening like a vise. I reread the fine print, searching for an error, a typo that would explain this impossible discrepancy. My hands started to tremble, the paper feeling suddenly brittle beneath my touch. I backed away from the desk, the sound of my own breathing loud in the quiet dorm room. Who was Elias Vance? And why did this scholarship, the culmination of years of relentless studying, belong to a ghost? A profound sense of disorientation washed over me, leaving my limbs heavy and unresponsive.

[story 2]

I stared at the acceptance email, the screen illuminating my wide, unblinking eyes. The figures were correct—my name, my GPA, the promised funding. Yet, the dedication line, the personalized note from the Dean, mentioned a different name, a name I didn't recognize. A sudden, sharp intake of breath hitched in my throat. I dropped the tablet onto the worn carpet, the clatter echoing unnervingly. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic, trapped bird. I began pacing the small room, the floorboards protesting under my quick, uneven steps. Each corner of the room seemed to press in, amplifying the frantic rhythm inside my chest. I needed an answer, a solid explanation, but the silence of the room offered only an echoing void.

[story 3]

When I finally managed to decipher the administrative file, the realization hit me like a physical blow. The funds, the prestigious opportunity, it wasn't mine. It was allocated to a student named Marcus Bell. A wave of nausea rolled over me, making my vision swim momentarily. I gripped the edge of the wooden table so tightly my knuckles turned white. I stood up abruptly, needing space, needing to move away from the evidence. My mouth felt dry and tight, and I could feel a distinct pressure building behind my eyes. I looked around the familiar library stacks, suddenly feeling exposed, as if every shadow held an observer waiting to witness this profound, unwelcome truth.
